item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Radioactivity isn't going to be a problem here.
https://www.wolframalpha.com/input/?i=%289.15E6kg*%28358mg%2F100g%29*1.14E-4%2F%2840grams%2Fmole%29*avagrados+constant*1.311MeV%29*ln%282%29%2F1.25E9years
.21mw of radiation given off.
This is the equivalent of 256 mili siverts a day. Would be lethal over a few weeks.
Then there's the fact that even if you waded throught the banana pulp you couldn't realistically absord even 1/10000 of the radiation, so it's not a hazard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Laughed at the fact that those guys were still caught in the end.
Even with their banana idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I trust dado +1

item.title: easy +1
item.content:encoded: So this is the birthplace of the now-infamous dado? I'm impressed it is so recent, relatively speaking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My maths puts it more at the level of a containment failure in a solid-source radiotherapy or possibly a radioisotope-labelled tracer scanning ward, than a reactor core. The radioactive material just isn't high enough activity, concentrated or directed enough to cause prompt harm even to those in the immediate vicinity. Whilst you'll want to get rid of it simply because having an isolated background radiation level is something that carries a small but nonzero stochastic and cumulative health risk to anyone in the area, the more pressing (literally) problem will be the sheer mass of fruit that's been suddenly injected into the environment. We're talking nearly ten thousand tonnes, and a thousand cubic metres. That much anything spontaneously appearing in an enclosed space will cause problems (or even in an occupied outdoor space).

Still, if you point a geiger counter at it, you'll definitely get a heightened reading. Lots of scints on a gamma camera scope. Etc. But the reading off either of those things has to be really high before there's an immediate threat to life. dado has the right idea in terms of overall fatal dose, but it would have to be contained within a single person, without significant self-shielding effects, for the usual amount of time that food stays in the body, to actually wreak the intended havoc.

(Same as the case for receiving actual damage from beta and particularly alpha particle emitters - those pretty much HAVE to be inside you to have an effect. Betas can cause some secondary damage from bremsstrahlung X-rays if their energy and activity are high enough, but Alphas need to be inside your body because even the epidermis is sufficient to block all but the most energetic examples… unlike the account I've seen elsewhere on the forums talking about Gamma Rays etc. An alpha source that will kill you dead in minutes if ingested will do diddly-squat outside of the body, other than maybe lightly burning the already-dead upper layers of exposed skin and, if you're not wearing goggles, irritating your eyes. However, if that means you go tear-blind, you could still crash a vehicle you were at the controls of. Secondary splash damage…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm, OK, so one banana is approximately 0.1 microsieverts. If memory serves, the typical average background radiation, as well as the recommended general public limit for additional artificial radiation exposure, is 1 millisievert, and for medical radiation workers it's 20 mSv.

Can't remember the fatal dosage, but perhaps we can assume it's 100x that or something, thus 2Sv. Or maybe even just 1Sv? There was a fairly wide safety margin, but not sure if it was a full 50x.

0.1 micro-Sv times 9.something million equals, well, nine point something sieverts. That's gotta be bad for you. Perhaps that's exactly the LD50 for short-term human radiation exposure? Meaning the safe limit per year is somewhat below 1/365th of the fatal one-day dose, just to be super careful.

+ show block
– hide block

edit: OK, 50mSv is the US radiation worker limit; 100mSv for those involved in emergency defence of property (also the level at which risk of developing fatal cancers is doubled), 250mSv for life-saving emergency work. 400mSv produces radiation sickness if absorbed short-term. 2Sv causes acute RS with potential fatal consequences if not treated, 4Sv is high risk of fatality without critical treatment, and 8Sv is guaranteed death. 50Sv is equivalent to standing next to the Chernobyl core for 10 minutes.

Question is, what length of time do the bananas release their Equivalent Dose over? Presumably it's the length of time their substance would be in the body, so probably a good 24 hours or more in most cases. Also, they're going to be somewhat spread out because of their sheer volume without being massive enough to suffer gravitational collapse; one banana is about 110cc's, so we're looking at about a million litres of banana smoothie. Which actually is only equivalent to a cube 10m on a side (I think I've calculated that correctly?), but still, it's not quite the same as a point source with the same activity, or even a half-melted reactor core. Like, you don't get serious radiation sickness images on boats carrying thousands of tonnes of the things. So it should be reasonably safe to get in, extract the affected personnel, and set about hurridly washing the stuff away, so long as you don't hang about, keep your distance, and limit your time spent actually in contact with the goo. As ever: time, distance, shielding. With the middle one partly taken care of by the activity being spread out, the former by the somewhat lower than otherwise expected instantaneous activity, and the latter by the thickness of the goo acting as a barrier to emissions from deeper layers.

It's neither a point nor directed source, so the radiation will be shooting off in all directions. If it was inside you, it'd be super bad news, but if you're simply near or even swimming in it, the great majority will be directed off elsewhere and you're not personally going to absorb that much. On top of which, if it takes, say, 24 hours to absorb 8Sv even if you were subject to the full force, then over the course of an hour you'd actually "only" receive 333mSv. Which might make you feel a bit icky, and could cause some minor long term damage, but it won't kill you outright. You can spend 10 minutes subject to the entire output and only just exceed the regular radiation worker's annual dose limit. In reality you'd probably receive maybe 1mSv per five minutes, making prolonged exposure a suboptimal but by no means rapidly injurious affair.

Still, it's going to be a mother of a problem to get rid of. And the poor bastard who swallowed the pill still has their stomach a splode, then gets drowned in/crushed by bananas unless they're in a fairly open space with room to receive 1000 cubic metres of banana (with them ending up at the top, if they happened to be face down when the deluge started).

Oh, and that's approximately 25,000 bananas per second, average. Peak flow presumably much higher. Even if the sheer weight of them doesn't get you, they're going to be emerging with lethal force.

dado: humongous and actually kinda threatening issues wrapped up in a disarmingly bonkers package.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is hilarious, and now I have a burning need to look up just how much radioactivity is given off by nearly 10,000 tonnes of banana… each single nanner has only a very tiny amount of radiation after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bananas are slightly radioactive, or to be precise, it's the potassium isotope within that's radioactive. Collect a massive amount of slightly radioactive material and voila, you have irradiated zone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it but why would the banana slurry be radioactive? I thought the bananas themselves aren't anomalous, just their materialisation is?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love these SCPs that are a tad silly but manage to be accepted mainlist articles. SCPs like this one, SCP-426, even SCP-3008 are amazing in my opinion. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: dado is good pharmacy, yes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: that's so intresting tbh i never knew about this

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
dado might be one of my new favorite characters to voice. God, I’m just so pleased that these are a thing.

https://youtu.be/iENLVb0vMDs

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …this is super-useful to know (that alt="text" will allow screen readers to describe images, which is useful for people who can't see the image itself). Thank you for bringing it up!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good call. I've made that edit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I like the header image, it had a serious accessibility problem: blind people can't see them. A screen reader will just read out the filename, which isn't useful at all.

You can fix this by adding an ALT attribute to the image tag.

[[Image filename.png alt="Level 2/3521 Classified. Item number: SCP-3521. Object class: Safe"]]

item.title: Re: Good read, but...
item.content:encoded:
I'm not a great fan of the picture that has the item number and class…

I've got some bad news for you there then, my man.

item.title: Good read, but...
item.content:encoded:
I'm not a great fan of the picture that has the item number and class…
I do have a little critique but I forgot what I was going to say, like man, I hate it.
Otherwise good read and good series. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Goddammit, dado.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: More Dado i love Dado and have Dado related scp ideas. this is hilarious but honestly works well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, it's this. Home boy was digesting those pills for thirty minutes or so, and then had himself a banana experience over the course of six and a half minutes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, it just means it took 30 minutes for the pills to take effect, then 6.5 minutes for all the bananas to manifest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Coming back to this, I still love it, but it mentions that during testing it took 6.5 minutes for the bananas to appear, but… that it also took 30 minutes?

item.title: Well.
item.content:encoded:
Now everyone's looking at me like I'm crazy for laughing.

Plus, Dado sounds like a shifty reality bender doing bad stuff for money. Just from those text messages alone, I already constructed an image in my head. Good writing.

Take your damn upvote, this is genius. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
gross crush trauma from 9.15 million kg of bananas

Lost it right about here. This is amazing, all hail dado.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Based on information recovered during the discovery of SCP-3521, it is believed this volume of bananas is intended to cause an acute lethal dose of ionizing radiation. While bananas do contain trace amounts of radioactive potassium, the quantity manifested induces the much more obvious causes of death of exsanguination, suffocation, or in most confirmed cases of SCP-3521: gross crush trauma from 9.15 million kg of bananas manifesting within the subject's stomach.

I had a legit LOL at that. I imagine some twitchy kid watching YouTube and getting the idea, testing it out, and they saying "good enough." +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Minions?
In my SCP Foundation?
It's more likely than you think.

Awesome article, and dado needs to be in something else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is, actually, a very clever weapon. Think about it. The radiation didn't killed the guy. It rendered an entire containment facility unrecoverable, and killed for good any potential survivors of the collapse.

And about discretion? What anyone can guess from the ruined building is that it was overload with bananas. What can we do with this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fuck, this is stupid and I love it. What a hilarious tragedy. I love the concept of a "para-pharmacologist".

I can just see some researcher or something trying to kill people with bananas, just failing over and over. "Why isn't the ionizing radiation killing them? I just don't understand!" And some executive's standing behind them, mouth-breathing, shouting, "Because there's too many fucking bananas you mangosteen!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: dado needs to return

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: who point finger at? grocery store?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh. Well, this is definitely different than your usual fare. I feel like this is juuuuust treading the line between interesting/bizarre and over-the-top, and rides mostly on the strange premise. And like seagull, I'm unsure about the header becoming a standard authorial lilt of yours.

Still, I'd say this amused me and is executed well enough to get a marginal +1.

item.title: Re: nanananananananananananananananabananabomb
item.content:encoded: can you make this into a series. with dado servicing various anomalous clientele with his hilarious antics? or just give him a cameo in something you make later. i love him

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a great example of horror being mined from absurdity. Happy to upvote this to +100! Congrats, djkaktus!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Should be tagged "radioative" too, no?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In soviet Russia, you do not explode banana, banana explode you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, yes, the crushing and the exploding with bananas is bad, but why doesn't anyone look on the bright side here? With that much potassium, you won't have to worry about getting a leg cramp for the rest of your life!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: djkaktus wasn't the one that added the tags.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you forgot the food tag, djkaktus

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "You see Ivan, when use 50 million banana as radioactive material, enemy will never detect you for seeing bananas as normal."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Shortly after consumption, an extremely large number of unpeeled bananas will begin to manifest in the subject's stomach at an indeterminate rate

Holy shit upvoted

Now to read the rest of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Praise dado. My life is 20% better with this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Trust dado. All hail dado. Dado texts exactly the same way i do, and seems to have a similarly as ridiculous thought process.+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted because this is great.

There's some tone issues though, I'll PM you laterrrrr. <3

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding this.

If your reason for downvoting an article is an image at the top of it, that's….odd to me.

You can dislike formatting quirks, but ignoring good writing for a pet peeve is petty in my eyes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dislike most of your work. Not because it’s poorly written- on the contrary, I think your writing is phenomenal. It’s just that the concepts are generally too complicated or long-winded for my personal tastes, and I don’t have it in me to wrap my head around them.

This was fun, easy to grasp, and very nicely presented. A very enthusiastic +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of those dumb ideas that needed excellent execution to pull off, and you nailed it. +1, man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yessa.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article says nothing of the subject needing to be human. They could use, say, lab rats or monkeys, or even use it as a gross form of euthanasia if human subjects end up being necessary. I don't think they'd have much problem with feeding people remains, though, since there's so little of it compared to the bananas. Unless they're worried about Kuru

+1 doe

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
there are 17 instances of the word "banana" in this article.
Don't go full bananas folks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean, seeing as the bananas killed the subject used to manifest it, and bananas coated in human innards isn't exactly hygienic, MCF part is a bit far-fetched to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The dialogue at the end was the best. We need more dado.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's funny, I recently saw a video on YouTube about obscure units of measurement and one of the ones mentioned is a unit for measuring radiation based on the amount of radiation you get exposed to from eating a banana. It is called the Banana Equivalent Dose. It is a useful unit because the amount of radiation in a banana is extremely tiny, just a small fraction of the radiation you normally get in one day, so it is a good thing to compare other low radiation things with.

See it here if you are interested.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"30,000 Pounds of Bananas" by Harry Chapin came to mind. This is, in its own understated way, one of the stranger SCPs I've read recently.

I'm imagining MCF trying to acquire some as a famine relief mechanism, while MC&D want it as a cruel assassination device.
